title: Contingency Management
subtitle: Work smarter, not harder, to achieve recovery
author: Ned

seo.title: Contingency Management | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Contingency Management. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
While eliminating substance and addictive problems leads to greater life satisfaction over the long-term, teens are particularly oriented toward short-term rewards. Giving up something from which they derive pleasure and relief can cause irritability, depression, and downright rebellion. We use Contingency Management to provide the motivational scaffolding that teens need to address addiction problems effectively.

Why isn’t recovery immediately rewarding for teens? Don’t they understand that substance use is bad for them?

There are a couple of reasons to explain this phenomenon.

For one, teens with substance problems are more likely to engage in something known as delayed discounting. Delayed discounting is when an individual perceives a small, immediate reward as more rewarding than a large, delayed reward. To a teen, the small, immediate reward of getting high might feel immediately more important than the potential long-term reward of getting good grades.

Similarly, teens with substance problems are more likely to perceive a small immediate consequence as more distressing than a larger, delayed consequence. The discomfort from being in withdrawal might feel worse to a teen than the idea of potentially disappointing their parents long term.

Parents often shelter their teens from experiencing the harshest immediate consequences of substance use. If an adult gets caught at work using substances, they will likely be fired. If a teen gets caught at school using substances, a parent is likely to step in and prevent them from being expelled, suspended, or even serving detention.

Though no one wants a teen to be expelled from school, sheltering teens leads to consequences. Teens learn that no matter what they do, their actions will never result in truly harmful consequences, an attitude that does not hold up in the adult world.

So how can you change a teen’s motivational landscape so that they no longer use substances? At Plan Your Recovery, we use a rewarding, adaptive, and evidence-based approach that’s rooted in the family system: contingency management.

The basics of contingency management: operant conditioning

Contingency management is built on the behavioral science of operant conditioning. First described by B.H Skinner in the mid 1900s, operant conditioning holds that behaviors can be modified or maintained by their responses. In describing the types of behavioral responses, Skinner determined four main categories:

  • positive reinforcement (used most often in contingency management)
  • negative reinforcement (used as a supplement when necessary, in contingency management)
  • positive punishment (almost never used in contingency management)
  • negative punishment (almost never used in contingency management)

The basics of these categories are below:

Positive reinforcement introduces a desired stimulus (a reward) to strengthen a behavior. Examples include:

  • Providing top-selling employees a financial bonus. Selling= the desired behavior, bonus= the reward
  • Giving a dog a treat when he comes when called. Responding to the call = the desired behavior, the treat = the reward.
  • Taking a child out for ice cream after she finishes her chores. Finishing chores = the desired behavior, ice cream = the reward.

In a nutshell: the recipient wants the reward; thus, they engage in the behavior.

Negative reinforcement removes a stimulus to strengthen a behavior. Examples include:

  • Going to bed early to avoid being tired the next day. Being tired = the unpleasant stimulus, going to bed = the desired behavior.
  • Carefully preparing for a meeting after feeling unprepared at the last meeting. Feeling unprepared = the unpleasant stimulus, preparing = the desired behavior.
  • Removing a pinch collar from a dog after he stops pulling on his leash. The discomfort of the pinch collar = the unpleasant stimulus, walking well on the leash = the desired behavior. In a nutshell: the recipient does not want to continue to experience the stimulus. Thus, the recipient engages in the behavior.

Positive punishment introduces an undesirable stimulus to weaken an undesired behavior. Examples include: Receiving a ticket after speeding. The ticket = the undesired stimulus, speeding = the undesired behavior. Assigning a child extra chores after they stayed out past curfew. The chores = the undesired stimulus, staying out past curfew = the undesirable behavior. A coach making an athlete run laps after the athlete arrived late to practice. The laps = the undesired stimulus, arriving late to practice = the undesirable behavior. In a nutshell: the recipient does not want to continue to experience the stimulus. Thus, the recipient ceases the behavior.

Negative punishment removes a desired stimulus to weaken an undesired behavior. Examples include: Not allowing a child to watch TV until they finish their homework. TV = the desired stimulus, watching TV instead of doing homework = the undesirable behavior. Lessening an employee's paycheck after he skipped for work several days. The full paycheck = the desired stimulus, skipping work = the undesirable behavior. Grounding a teen after he got in a fight. The freedom to leave the house = the desired stimulus, fighting = the undesirable behavior. In a nutshell: the recipient of the punishment wants the desired stimulus back. Thus, they cease the behavior.

Beyond operant conditioning: how contingency management works.

As described above, contingency management is a primarily rewarding, rather than punishing, intervention. It scaffolds motivation to foster long term behavioral change and relies most heavily on Skinner’s first category of reinforcement: positive reinforcement.

Finding something that is desired by your teen is essential in developing a contingency management plan. There are three important considerations when determining this reinforcer.

  1. the rate of reinforcement (i.e. when that reward will be delivered following the presence of a desired behavior)
  2. the second is the size of the reinforcer (how rewarding that reinforcement will be)
  3. the schedule of reinforcement (i.e. how frequent will that reward be delivered).

All of these factors influence the likelihood and frequency that a behavior occurs.

The two most common forms of rewards in contingency management are voucher-based reinforcement and prize incentives.

Voucher based reinforcement provides a voucher for a desired behavior such as a negative urine test or attendance to treatment. That voucher holds a predetermined value that can then be exchanged for a reward of their choosing. By rewarding teens each time they demonstrate their commitment to sobriety, they have the chance to collect tangible benefits to use for a larger prize, or goal.

Price incentives are also beneficial with teens. Prizes are more immediate rewards that result from desired behavior. Because prizes are immediate, they are often used in conjunction with voucher-based reinforcement so that individuals can experience immediate benefits of sobriety while also having something larger and long term to look forward to.

Though rewards in the form of an item or an activity can be rewarding, rewards need not only be something that can be purchased. In fact, rewards may be most motivating when they come in the form of an earned privilege or freedom. For example, we find that a lot of our younger clients are motivated by the opportunity to get their license or earn back privileges that they lost because of their substance use. The opportunity to gain more freedom enhances motivation and also allows the client to demonstrate their ability to handle more freedom.

Punishment

Contingency management rarely uses punishments to decrease undesirable behaviors. While punishment does alter behavior, it can lead to secrecy and deception that interferes with engagement in treatment. Furthermore, if behaviors are changed in an attempt to avoid negative consequences, that change becomes less intrinsic. It may stop after the removal of the punishment or if punishment can be avoided through deception.

For this reason, punishment should only be used sparingly and with thoughtful consideration. Instead of punishment, undesirable behaviors (i.e. positive screens) result in the withholding of the reinforcer until that positive behavior returns.

Contingency management in action.

After the initial assessment, we ask that families come into treatment, and we outline the approach. It will work like this:

First, we determine the desirable behavior. Say for example, a teen vapes regularly and binge drinks with their friends on the weekends. The goal of treatment as determined by teen and their parents is the elimination substance use. Therefore, the desirable behavior would be abstinence from substances.

Next, we would determine how to measure abstinence. The most accurate way to measure whether or not someone used is through screening. But in order to determine full abstinence, testing must be spaced in a way that ensures complete detection. It is the role of the contingency plan to eliminate any window where use would be undetected, especially at the beginning of treatment. For example, because the detection period for vaping and drinking is about 72 hours, we would recommend 2-3 tests per week on average, decreasing as the teen makes progress.

The next step is to determine the appropriate reinforcer for the desirable behavior. We have found that it generally works best to use multiple types of rewards to make recovery positive and reinforcing. For example, clients should regularly receive some sort of reinforcement for negative screening. This could mean small prizes, such as ice cream or a privilege, or could be small amounts of money (or vouchers) that they can collect and use for something larger.

Just as important as smaller, aggregate reinforcement is the establishment of a long-term goal, as it is beneficial to see recovery not just as a daily effort but a long-term lifestyle change. This should be done by establishing a specific, desired award that they will receive at a predetermined period of sobriety.

Our contingency management approach is a positive, reinforcing intervention that intentionally works to reward the client as opposed to punish them. While we do not typically include any punishment in our approach, negative reinforcement can be an appropriate response to undesirable behavior. What is critical, however, is that the negative reinforcement must be determined in advance. The teen must understand ahead of time that if X occurs, Y will occur as a consequence.

The most common kind of negative reinforcement that is used in our contingency management approach is the absence of positive incentives. This may seem fairly obvious but when someone tests positive for their substance of choice, it is important that they not receive their prize or voucher incentive.

Additionally, if their reward is that they get to use their car, it would be appropriate to eliminate their car privileges for a predetermined amount of time. If a client has been working towards earning their car but haven’t yet reached this milestone, a predetermined amount of time may be added to that date.

All and all, the most important part of negative reinforcement is that it is determined in advance, and everyone is aware of what the result of using is. Establishing this from the onset allows the client to know exactly what the result of their behavior will be and make a well-informed decision accordingly.
